claim: Candidate names high-load services and transactional logic in fintech as key experience
quote: I worked closely with high-load services, message queues, and generally with money, meaning with transactional logic, where a mistake is costly

claim: Five years of Python experience, three years in fintech, currently mid-plus developer at Roltech
quote: I've been writing Python for five years, three of those specifically in fintech, right now I work at a company called Roltech as a mid-plus developer

claim: Strong STAR: incident with the payment authorization service failing last March
quote: we had a case in March last year, the payment authorization service started failing under load during peak hours

claim: Strong STAR: action — found N+1 query, rewrote to batch fetching, added Redis cache with 30s TTL
quote: found that we had an N+1 query to the database on every authorization, rewrote that piece to use batch fetching and added a Redis cache with a thirty-second TTL

claim: Strong STAR: measurable result — p99 latency dropped from 1200 to 180 ms, timeouts fell almost to zero
quote: p99 latency dropped from 1200 milliseconds to 180 milliseconds, and the number of timeouts in production went down to practically zero

claim: Weakly verifiable STAR: microservices migration, described impersonally through 'we', without the candidate's specific role
quote: Well, we all generally worked on it together, I was involved too, I wrote part of the code, I attended the architecture calls

claim: Weakly verifiable STAR: candidate refuses to name a personal contribution to the migration, no measurable personal result
quote: I'm not ready to say that this particular service is personally mine, we all moved the project forward together

claim: Motivation to leave — hit a growth ceiling, no senior position at the current job
quote: I've hit a growth ceiling, there just isn't a senior position there, the headcount is small, the next step up is team lead, and I'm not ready to manage people yet, I want to grow specifically as an engineer

claim: Wants more autonomy in architectural decisions, currently everything is decided from above
quote: I want more autonomy in architectural decisions, right now a lot gets decided from above, without my input

claim: First 90 days: get familiar with the system, take on a medium task, by the third month lead the performance work on the payment service
quote: by the third month I'd like to already be leading a separate area, for example the performance of the payment service

claim: Expects to participate in design reviews for new services, not just writing code against a ready-made spec
quote: participating in design reviews for new services, not just writing code against a ready-made spec

claim: Failure: a billing release led to double-charging about 5% of customers
quote: after the release about five percent of customers got double-charged

claim: Candidate acknowledges his own technical shortcoming — didn't account for a race condition in the idempotency locking logic
quote: I didn't account for a race condition on request retries in rare cases, there was a window of a few milliseconds where two requests could pass the idempotency check at the same time, that's my shortcoming in the locking logic

claim: Candidate shifts responsibility onto external circumstances — management's tight deadlines and the infrastructure team's config change
quote: QA didn't have time to run the full regression because of the tight deadlines management set for us, and on top of that the infrastructure team changed the queue config right before the release, without warning

claim: Candidate's question about the code review process and number of approvers for a merge
quote: how is your code review process set up, how many approvers are needed to merge into master

claim: Candidate's question about balancing feature velocity against tech debt, personally important to him
quote: what's your approach to balancing feature velocity against tech debt, is there dedicated time for refactoring, or does product decide everything

claim: Salary expectations: range of 350-400 thousand rubles take-home
quote: I'm expecting a range from three hundred fifty to four hundred thousand rubles take-home

claim: Start timeline — three weeks after the offer (two-week notice period plus a week off)
quote: realistically three weeks after the offer

claim: Work format — hybrid, two-three days in the office
quote: I prefer hybrid, two-three days in the office, the rest remote

claim: SPECIFIC FACT: the latency episode is confirmed with numbers and documented by a postmortem in Jira — verifiable
quote: it was filed as a separate incident in Jira, there's a postmortem with graphs, I can attach the link if you need it for a reference

claim: GENERAL DECLARATION: the microservices migration episode has no specific role and no numbers — not verifiable
quote: I can't give exact numbers right now, I'd need to check the dashboard, but subjectively it grew noticeably, probably several times over

claim: In the latency episode, the candidate attributes the result to himself (the main implementation and solution is his)
quote: the main implementation and solution — that's mine, yes

claim: In the migration episode, the result is attributed to the team, the candidate's role is blurred
quote: we all moved the project forward together

claim: UNSTABLE: mixed locus of responsibility for the failure — acknowledges his own technical mistake but then shifts the main blame to external deadlines and a config change
quote: if we'd been given a reasonable testing timeline and the queue config hadn't been touched at the last moment, we would have caught it back on staging

claim: Role fit: direct experience with high-load payment services and transactional logic matches the payments team job profile
quote: the payment authorization service started failing under load during peak hours

claim: Risk/ambiguity: willingness to relocate is undetermined, the candidate gives no clear yes or no
quote: I can't say a clear yes or no right now, I'd need to understand the details on relocation compensation

claim: Risk: the candidate has a parallel process with an offer in a week and a half to two weeks — a tight decision window
quote: they said they'd give an offer in about a week and a half to two weeks, so that's a reference point for my timeline too

claim: Main insight: the candidate confirms his experience with high-load payment services with a strong, verifiable episode, but the second STAR is weakly verifiable and there is a risk of blurred self-attribution on failure; recommendation — request a reference from the previous job and clarify the personal contribution to the migration
quote: the main implementation and solution — that's mine, yes
